coredata.dc:description: This review explores the evolution and application of fluorescence sensors based on quantum dots (QDs) for detecting environmental and biological analytes across diverse real-world scenarios and complex sample matrices and also categorizes different types of quantum dots, such as carbon dots (C-dots), graphene quantum dots (GQDs), and metal-doped QDs and examines their properties, including tunable fluorescence, low toxicity, and photostability, which make them ideal for a variety of applications. Key sensing mechanisms, including Förster Resonance Energy Transfer (FRET) and fluorescence quenching, are discussed alongside innovations like paper-based, ratiometric, and turn-on/turn-off sensors. Additionally, case studies are provided to showcase the application of these sensors in environmental and biomedical fields, where they provide rapid, sensitive, and cost-effective solutions. This review presents the potential of quantum dot-based fluorescence sensors to transform analytical detection technologies, offering new opportunities in environmental monitoring, bioimaging, and public health safety.

head.ce:title: Advances in quantum dot-based fluorescence sensors for environmental and biomedical detection

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This review explores the evolution and application of fluorescence sensors based on quantum dots (QDs) for detecting environmental and biological analytes across diverse real-world scenarios and complex sample matrices and also categorizes different types of quantum dots, such as carbon dots (C-dots), graphene quantum dots (GQDs), and metal-doped QDs and examines their properties, including tunable fluorescence, low toxicity, and photostability, which make them ideal for a variety of applications. Key sensing mechanisms, including Förster Resonance Energy Transfer (FRET) and fluorescence quenching, are discussed alongside innovations like paper-based, ratiometric, and turn-on/turn-off sensors. Additionally, case studies are provided to showcase the application of these sensors in environmental and biomedical fields, where they provide rapid, sensitive, and cost-effective solutions. This review presents the potential of quantum dot-based fluorescence sensors to transform analytical detection technologies, offering new opportunities in environmental monitoring, bioimaging, and public health safety.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: The pursuit of industrial and biotechnological advancement has significantly contributed to rising environmental contamination issues worldwide. The controlled or uncontrolled release of hazardous pollutants from various industrial sectors is one of the key problems facing humanity. Among them, adverse influences of lead, cadmium, and mercury on human health are well known to cause many disorders like reproductive, neurological, endocrine system, and cardiovascular, etc. Besides their presence at lower concentrations, most of these toxic heavy metals are posing noteworthy toxicological concerns. In this context, notable efforts from various regulatory authorities, the increase in the concentration of these toxic heavy metals in the environment is of serious concern, so real-time monitoring is urgently required [1].

On the other hand, physical health is an eternal theme of humanity and has always been widely concerned and discussed. However, some diseases, especially cancer, pose a serious threat to human health. Accordingly, the development and popularization of precise but efficient early diagnosis means for diseases are conducive to implementing early diagnosis and treatment strategies and save the lives of most patients, which is the motivation for researchers to specialize in early diseases diagnosis [2].

Numerous companies globally manufacture various compounds that might adversely affect the environment, human health, and living species, necessitating identification and remediation. Fluorescence spectroscopy has developed into a potent analytical method for the selection and highly sensitive identification of diverse samples, including heavy metal ions [3].

Quantum dots (QDs) are semiconductor nanocrystals that exhibit unique optical and electronic properties due to their nanoscale dimensions. These properties make them versatile tools in various applications, including environmental monitoring, biomedical diagnostics, and optoelectronic devices. QDs can be categorized into several types based on their composition and structure, such as carbon dots (C-dots), graphene quantum dots (GQDs), and metal-doped QDs [4].

Some examples of optical sensors that utilize fluorescence-based principles include Quenchable fluorophores, chromogenic and fluorogenic reagents, biosensers, Förster Resonance Energy Transfer (FRET), and surface plasmon resonance mechanisms [5–8]. These sensors provide versatile and efficient tools for the analysis and monitoring of a variety of target analytes. The use of fluorescent probes in monitoring systems is steadily increasing because of its user-friendliness, high specificity, and cost efficiency [9–12]. In addition to high sensitivity and simplicity, fluorescence-based methods have gained growing interest due to their ease of use. They also offer excellent selectivity, with minimal interference from colored components [13].

Fluorescence-based sensing relies on the binding between a fluorophore molecule and the target analyte. This interaction induces modifications in the fluorescence properties, including lifetime, intensity, and ansiotropy. These changes are directly related to the energy or charge transfer processes occurring between the fluorophore and the analyte [14]. Among all types of fluorescent sensors, carbon dots (C-dots) which are inorganic nanomaterials with fluorescent properties have attracted significant attention because of their affordability, low toxicity, and great fluorescence properties [15–18]. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), typically sized under 10 nm, demonstrate intense fluorescence emission, making them superior choice over conventional semiconductor quantum dots (QDs) and organic dyes. They are advantageous due to their low toxicity, environmental sustainability, cost-effectiveness, and straightforward synthesis methods [18–20]. Thanks to the exceptional electronic, optical and biochemical characteristics, C-dots have gained significant attention in a variety of applications. These include electrocatalysis [21,22], bioimaging [23], sensing [24], electrochemiluminescence [25], and drug or gene delivery [26], as well as various other optoelectronic domains [27]. The advancement of fluorescence measurements has created a reliable tool for both quantitative and qualitative screening in detection applications [28]. Fluorescence-based probes have emerged as a vital component in analytical techniques, offering unmatched sensitivity and simplicity [29–31].

In light of the increasing reliance on QD-based fluorescence systems for real-time monitoring, several recent studies have demonstrated their effectiveness in diverse applications. For instance, Zhang et al. developed a particularly sensitive fluorescent assay to detect carbofuran using a time-resolved fluorescence strip with dual labeling [32]. Similarly, Majid et al. used graphene oxide (GO) sheets alongside l-cysteine-capped ZnS QDs to create an aptasensor utilizing resonance energy transfer (RET) to detect the fungicide edifenphos [33]. Additionally, Wu et al. used CQDs to develop a fluorescence RET sensor to monitor organophosphorus pesticides (OPs) [34]. Optoelectronic-responsive organic-inorganic nanohybrid fluorescence sensors highlight the increasing interest in this domain and its potential for novel applications [35]. Several approaches, including high-performance liquid chromatography [36], gas chromatography/mass spectrometry [37], electrochemical analysis [38] and colorimetric assays [39], have been designed for identifying OPs. However, these methods are often limited by low sensitivity, lengthy analysis times, and requirements for skilled personel, which can also lead to false positives.

In recent years, fluorescent biosensors have proven to be a viable alternative for the quick and sensitive detection of OPs. By leveraging fluorescence readout and photo-excitation-emission, these biosensors offer far lower background signals and much higher sensitivity, overcoming the shortcomings of conventional techniques [40–44]. To capitalize on this potential, several fluorescent probes have been created by researchers using ratiometric or off-on techniques. Metallic nanoclusters [40,45], semiconductor QDs [46], C-dots [47] and organic materials [44] have been used as signaling probes in cascade catalytic reactions to detect OPs. Among these, biosensors that inhibit acetylcholinesterase (AChE) activity have also shown great promise for OP detection, with applications spanning multiple fields [45–47]. Despite this progress, designing fluorescence sensors for specific OPs remains challenging due to inherent issues including complex fabricaton process, self-quenching/bleaching, weak luminescence, and poor photostability [48]. Lu et al. has sought to address these limitations, with notable examples including Lu et al. use of a luminescent dinuclear Ir(III) complex [49], and Yan et al.'s investigation of fluorescent OP detection using manganese dioxide (MnO2) nanosheet-carbon dot systems [50].

This paper focuses on recent advances in quantum dot-based fluorescence sensors, aiming to provide a clear picture of their capabilities and limitations. It delves into a detailed analysis of their principles of operation and technical innovations, emphasizing their role in tackling critical challenges such as precise and timely monitoring of environmental pollutants and efficient detection of complex diseases.

Additionally, the paper explores future perspectives for the development of these sensors, including improvements in sensitivity, reduction of production costs, and enhancement of their stability under various conditions. It also examines the potential integration of these sensors with smart systems such as the Internet of Things, enabling advanced diagnostic and monitoring tools. These analyses aim to offer valuable guidance for future research and innovations in the field. To provide a structured overview of the various quantum dot types discussed throughout this review, their key features, sensing mechanisms, and representative applications are summarized in Table 1 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: QD design principles and sensing mechanisms

ce:section.ce:para: Quantum dots (QDs), a class of semiconductor nanoparticles, have attracted significant attention due to their outstanding optical and electrical capabilities. Possessing a broad range of excitation spectra, precise emission spectra, and remarkable photostability, QDs have proven to be highly effective fluorescent probes for detecting variety of substances, including amino acids, glucose, DNA, and heavy metals. Furthermore, nano-sized porphyrins exhibit unique structural and physicochemical properties that have positioned them as valuable assets in fields such as photosensitive materials, drug development, clinical oncology, and analytical detection. In substance detection, the combined use of QDs and nano porphyrins shows remarkable promise, providing a synergistic method with enormous potential to transform analytical research and application. As research in this area continues to progress, the integration of these nanoparticles is poised to revolutionize substance detection methodologies and open up opportunities for innovation in the field [51], where they prove to be invaluable as fluorescent probes due to their remarkable luminescent characteristics. QDs exhibit several advantageous properties, such as exceptional photostability, strong resistance to photobleaching, exceptional fluorescence efficiency, a sharp emission profile, a wide excitation spectrum, and the capacity to fine-tune their emission wavelength by adjusting size and composition [52–57].

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), are among the diverse materials derived from graphene, exhibit outstanding characteristics. These include excellent dispersibility in aqueous solutions, biocompatibility, high photostability, reduced toxicity levels, and remarkable thermal and chemical stability [58,59]. The distinct characteristics of GQDs have various practical uses in diverse domains such as bioimaging [60], light emitting diodes technology [61], photovoltaics [62], photocatalysis [63], chemical sensors [64] and biosensors [65]. Traditional QDs typically utilize Cd or Pb components and emit fluorescence visible to the human eye. However, their application in bioimaging and biodetection has been constrained by issues of low sensitivity, toxicity, and limited tissue penetration by their emitted fluorescence. The emergence of near-infrared (NIR) QDs has revolutionized this field by overcoming these challenges. While previously reported NIR QDs like PbS, CdTe, and InAs, containing toxic metals such as As (III), Cd (II), or Pb (II), have faced limitations in biological applications, the advent of ternary I-III-VI NIR QDs has presented a new avenue of exploration. These ternary QDs, including compositions like Cu–In–Se, CuInS2, and AgInS2, incorporate non-toxic metal elements, addressing concerns surrounding the toxicity of traditional QDs. This breakthrough in quantum dot technology offers promising opportunities for enhanced bioimaging capabilities and biodetection systems, setting the stage for innovative applications in the biological and medical fields [66–68].

Nevertheless, adjusting their chemical makeup proved to be laborious [69]. The I-VI Ag-based (Ag2S, Ag2Se, Ag2Te) QDs represent a novel category of NIR QDs characterized by a small band-gap with minimal toxicity. Featuring an intinsic band-gap ranging from 0.9 to 1.1 eV, Ag2S QDs emerge as promising contenders for NIR QDs, often heralded as exemplary nanomaterials for fluorescence-based bioimaging and biosensing [70]. Du et al. were the pioneers in reporting the synthesis of NIR Ag2S QDs [71]. Subsequent studies applied the resultant high-luminance second NIR region Ag2S QD for imaging lymphatic vessels and angiography in tumor treatment involving mice [72,73]. In recent studies, researchers utilized Ag2S QDs, known for their high biocompatibility and chemical stability, to label human mesenchymal stem cells (hMSCs). This labeling technique facilitated in vivo visualization of the transplanted stem cells, including their location, survival, and differentiation [74]. Shu et al. developed a NIR fluorescent sensor utilizing a low toxicicity Ag2S QD and nanosheet-based MnO2 architecture for detecting glutathione (GSH) within human serum samples [69].

A wide range of techniques has been implemented to enhance the surface characteristics of QDs for the advancement of nanoscale sensors. The primary goal of these surface modifications is to enhance the optical features, increase stability, and reduce any potential toxicity, particularly for use in the fields of biochemistry and biomedicine. Furthermore, these alterations are frequently customized to achieve precise levels of selectivity [75]. A variety of compounds, such as mercaptopropionic acid [76], thioglycolic acid [77], and various polymeric materials [78], have been utilized to transform the surface properties of QDs. A noteworthy practice involves the surface modification of nanomaterials through the integration of polysaccharide biopolymers, which has found extensive application in diverse biomedical scenarios owing to their exceptional biological attributes, which include biodegradability, compatibility with biological systems, lack of harmful effects, and ease of procurement [79–81]. As a pioneering example, researchers developed an optical nanosensor capable of detecting the small molecule tetracycline hydrochloride. This sensor utilized surface-modified CdTe/ZnS QDs by capping them with a biopolymer-based material. Specifically, starch (OS-g-PDMAEMA) grafted with poly (2-dimethylaminoethyl) methacrylate oxidized was employed as the capping agent through a straightforward methodology [82].

In their research, Wang et al. employed a one-step green hydrothermal process to produce C-dots using orange peels as precursor materials. Their investigation explored the correlation between volatile oils and quantum yield across 14 distinct orange peel varieties. Ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA) was then added as a chelating ligand to the resultant C-dots. Compared to neat C-dots, the EDTA-modified C-dots exhibited heightened sensitivity in detecting hexavalent chromium (Cr (VI)). This change simplified the development of a fluorescence sensor system for water sample Cr (VI) detection. Furthermore, the EDTA modified C-dots/Cr (VI) system was adapted as an “off-on” fluorescent sensor to detect ascorbic acid (AA). Remarkably, this sensor successfully quantified AA levels in both fresh oranges samples and commercially available orange juices [83].

Investigation of graphene as an individual layer of graphite began with P. Wallace's groundbreaking work in the early 1940s [84]. The concept of 'graphene' was originally introduced by Mouras et al. [85]. Graphene Lately, GQDs have gained substantial attention for their applications in biosensing and cell imaging, alongside their prospective utilization across various domains within the medical and pharmaceutical sectors [86]. These materials have garnered more attention due to their unusual edge structure effects, physicochemical characteristics, and quantum-confined electronic states [87], as well as their captivating optical characteristics, exceptional photostability, non-toxic nature, biocompatibility, and nanometer-scale lateral dimensions [88]. GQDs possess outstanding photoluminescent characteristics, which can be modulated by factors such as structural defects and surface functionalization, including the incorporation of heteroatoms [89]. Molecular interaction sites are inherent in GQDs, characterized by their rich complement of functional groups on the edges and surfaces [90]. This unique feature has garnered significant attention recently due to their low toxicity and facile customization, leading to a shift away from traditional inorganic semiconductor QDs [91]. The, characterized as a material with no band-gap, features an exciton Bohr radius that extends infinitely [92]. GQDs represent tiny graphene-based nanostructures with electron confinement in all three dimensions. Typically sized below 20 nm, these nanoparticles function akin to semiconductor nanocrystals, showcasing exciton restriction in all spatial dimensions. Essentially, GQDs are diminutive graphene fragments manifesting exciton restriction and quantum effects owing to their reduced size [91]. Besides their diverse applications, particularly in water, GQDs, considered a subset of C-dots, distinguish themselves with superior crystalline structures compared to their counterparts. They are consistently recognized for their distinctive electrical, mechanical, and optical properties [93,94].

Owing to the infinite exciton Bohr radius in graphene, GQDs display a quantum confinement effect that induces a finite bandgap. When excited, this effect generates luminescence, the properties of which can be tuned by modifying the size and surface features of the QDs [93]. Safardoust-Hojaghan et al. introduced an innovative method fo detection of AA. Initially, they synthesized a highly luminescent sulfur and nitrogen-doped GQDs (S,N-GQDs) using a hydrothermal process, employing citric acid as the carbon source and thiourea as the nitrogen, and sulfur sources. The resulting S,N-GQDs served as a photoluminescence sensor for detecting AA, functioning via an "off-on" detecting mechanism [95]. Shehab et al. synthesized GQDs via glucose carbonization. These GQDs were employed as the active material in the development of a non-enzymatic glucose sensor. The resulting sensor demonstrated a linear behavior across glucose concentrations within the range from 4 to 40 mM [96]. Liu et al. employed orange-emitting GQDs for AA detection. When hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) and horse radish peroxidase (HRP) are present, hydroxyl radicals oxidize catechol, turning it into o-benzoquinone. This oxidation process significantly quenches the fluorescence response of GQDs. Nevertheless, AA inhibits this quenching by consuming a portion of H2O2 and hydroxyl radicals. These findings demonstrate the constructed sensor ability to detect AA within a concentration range of 1.11–300 μM and a detection capability as low as 0.32 μM [97].

C-dots are a class of nanoparticles with diameters under 10 nm, exhibiting a quasi-spherical morphology. These nanoparticles possess great solubility in a wide range of solvents, along with favorable biocompatibility and low toxicity profiles [91]. The unique surface chemistry of C-dots, featuring carboxyl (─COOH) and hydroxyl (─OH) functional groups, enables the formation of hydrogen bonds with water molecules, ensuring their excellent solubility in aqueous environments. However, to further enhance biocompatibility, researchers are exploring additional functionalization strategies, including the use of polyethylene glycol (PEG) or other functional groups [98]. Following their unexpected discovery by Scrivens et al., C-dots have drawn substantial interest due to their application versatility, which includes energy conversion, photocatalysis, bioimaging, drug delivery, sensing, and optoelectronics. In the ultraviolet (UV) range, C-dots typically show strong optical absorption that extends into the visible spectrum, with adjustable fluorescence characteristics that vary depending on the excitation wavelength [91]. This tunability, combined with their biocompatibility and solubility, makes C-dots an appealing material for a variety of applications. Fluorescent C-dots have attracted considerable interest from researchers due to their distinctive and tunable optical properties, which are influenced by their size. Additionally, C-dots exhibit low toxicity, high biocompatibility, exceptional water solubility, simple functionalization, and enhanced photostability [99,100]. C-dots a recently emerged class of carbonaceous nanomaterials, were initially documented by Xu et al., in 2004 while using the arc-discharge method to separate single-walled carbon nanotubes (SWCNTs). C-dots have been the focus of extensive research for their fascinating and distinctive beneficial properties since their discovery [101–104]. Comparing to the fluorescent organic dyes and QDs, C-dots offer a variety of benefits including exceptional chemical stability, resistance to photobleaching, customizable luminescence emissions, and superior biocompatibility [100]. C-dots, with sizes under 10 nm, showcase notable photoluminescence properties along with low toxicity and molecular weight, chemical stability, and high solubility in water [105]. In recent times, carbogenic dots have displayed potential in various sectors including bio-imaging [106–108], energy efficiency [109], light-emitting devices [110], sensing [111], photocurrent response activities [112], and photocatalytic processes [113]. Recent studies have presented the potential of passivated C-dots in two-photon fluorescence imaging. For instance, Sun and coworkers successfully employed C-dots for optical imaging in live mice, highlighting their effectiveness in this application [114,115]. Two general categories can be used to describe the synthesis of C-dots: bottom-up and top-down methods [116,117]. The top-down approach involves breaking down larger carbon-based materials, including graphite or lamp black, through techniques like electro-oxidation [118], acid-assisted chemical oxidation [119], laser ablation [120]. However, this method often requires intricate processes, yields low quantities of C-dots, and demands strict synthesis conditions [121]. Conversely, the bottom-up approach provides a more straightforward and efficient way to produce C-dots, involving the carbonization of precursor molecules like amino acids [122,123], polyethylene glycol [124], and polyethyleneimine [125] through solvothermal synthesis.

A subclass of C-dots known as nitrogen-doped C-dots (NC-dots) have drawn growing interest because of their unique properties. The synthesis of NC-dots can be classified into three main groups based on the nitrogen source used: (1) employing small organic molecules including l-DOPA, amino acids, or ammonium citrate; (2) using inorganic molecules like aqueous ammonia or NaNH2; and (3) deriving NC-dots from natural sources such as strawberry juice [126], grass [127], bombyxmori silk [128]. NC-dots have found applications in various fields, including biosensing [129], gas adsorption (e.g., CO2 capture) [130], and the oxygen reduction reaction [131]. Metal ion detection using fluorescence sensors based on C-dots relies on electron transfer processes facilitated by chelation interactions between the functional groups and metal ions on the CDs surface, leading to changes in fluorescence intensity [132]. The presence of active amino (NH2) and –SH groups on the CDs surface that have an affinity for the targeted metal ions is what allows these sensors to detect particular metal ions [133,134]. Sulfur/nitrogen co-doped C-dots, known for their high quantum yield, play a crucial role in providing these active groups necessary for metal ion recognition [135,136].

While there are sparse instances of metal ions quenching C-dots for the purpose of metal ion detection, a programmable fluorescent sensor engineered to detect Hg2+ employed the concept of ligand-metal binding to trigger changes in fluorescence [137]. Utilizing silver ions (Ag+) as a mediator, scientists engineered sulfur-functionalized carbon dots (HS–C-dots) as a fluorescent sensor for detecting mercury ions (Hg2+) shown in Fig. 1 . Initially, the fluorescence of the C-dots was suppressed by Ag+ because of HS- C-dots/Ag aggregate formation. By dispersing HS- C-dots/Ag in a phosphate buffer saline medium, a "signal-on" fluorescent sensor was developed. The fluorescence of this sensor being reactivated upon the introduction of Hg2+. Through the replacement of Ag+ with Hg2+ in the HS- C-dots/Ag complex, which led to the formation of an amalgam, the sensor achieved an exceedingly low limit of detection for Hg2+ [138].

Das et al. present a straightforward technique for making water-based, green luminous NC-dots with l-arginine and lemon juice. When Cu(II) ions are introduced into the NC-dots solution in water, the fluorescence intensity of the solution is reduced because of an electron transfer process. Interaction between NC-dots surface NH2 groups and Cu(II) ions forms cupric amine complexes, leading to decreased fluorescence because of inner filter effect (IFE) depicted in Fig. 2 . This fluorescence modulation by Cu(II) ions has enabled the development of a highly selective and efficient detection platform for Cu(II) ions, showcasing innovative applications in the field of advanced chemistry [139].

Wang et al. developed an innovative fluorescence sensor combining magnetic covalent organic frameworks (COFs), molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs), and C-dots (MCOFs@MIPs@C-dots). This sensor integrates Fe3O4 nanoparticles for magnetic functionality, COFs for structural support and sorption properties, MIPs for selective absorption, and C-dots for fluorescence-based detection. Its efficacy was evaluated using 2,4,6-trinitrophenol (TNP) as the target compound [140]. It is noteworthy to highlight that COFs, a new category of porous crystalline polymers, feature covalently bonded networks with a periodic and extended structural arrangement. These frameworks are constructed by covalently linking various building blocks to form the interconnected architecture [141]. Li et al. developed a new method to produce a water-based fluorescent dual authentication ink using a dual excitation mechanism involving UV and NIR wavelengths. The ink consisted of CQDs enriched with rare earth elements ytterbium (Yb) and erbium (Er), which were effectively bound to nano-fibrillated cellulose (NFC) via a reductive amination process. NFC was then incorporated into the ink due to its unique rheological characteristics. Examination of the ink's thixotropy and curing dynamics illustrated that NFC, serving as a carrier for CQDs, could be smoothly incorporated into water-based ink, leading to a substantial enhancement in its rheological attributes [142].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Carbon dots based on inner filter effect
ce:section.ce:para: Akhgari et al. recently developed an efficient and sensitive fluorescence-based technique for Cefixime detection, which is among the most commonly used antibiotics. This approach leverages the inner filter effect (IFE) mechanism, which is a novel concept in this context. Utilizing the IFE mechanism to detect cefixime, their platform employed fluorescent C-dots as the fluorophore, which were created using a pot-method [143]. Contrary to its traditional reputation as a source of error in spectrofluorometric measurements, the IFE has been rediscovered as a valuable tool for optical chemical sensors. When the absorption spectrum of a species coincides with either the excitation or emission spectrum of a fluorophore, the IFE phenomenon arises, leading to a perceived reduction in emitted light intensity. Recent studies have shown that by harnessing this effect, IFE can be used to convert analytical absorption signals into fluorescence signals, making it a promising strategy for optical chemical sensing [144–146]. In this context, the IFE phenomenon can be employed to design novel and sensitive analytical methods, where the interactions between the fluorophore and the analyte is mediated by the IFE mechanism. Cefixime has been effectively detected using this method, highlighting the potential of IFE-based sensors for a variety of analytical uses. In sensors based on the IFE, it is unnecessary to establish a covalent bond between the fluorophore and the receptor; instead, both components are used in their native forms. Moreover, since variations in the absorber's absorbance result in exponential changes in the fluorophore's fluorescence, the analytical method is expected to exhibit enhanced sensitivity and lower detection limits compared to relying solely on absorbance measurements [147]. The IFE in fluorescence arises when quenching species (absorbers) within the detection system absorb the excitation or emission light from fluorophores, which happens when the absorber's absorption band overlaps with these spectra, even without a direct chemical connection between them [144,148,149]. Controlling the IFE is straightforward and affordable. In contrast to other fluorescence quenching techniques, the IFE has recently drawn attention as an effective method for creating new assays by transforming analytical absorption values into fluorescence signals to improve sensitivity and selectivity [150–152].

C-dots and GQDs are sensitive fluorescent nanomaterials that can be tailored to interact with specific target analytes, enhancing the competition between the analyte and quencher agents. This interaction triggers a change in the spectroscopic properties of the fluorescent nanomaterials, typically through reversible non-covalent interactions or irreversible covalent bonding [153–155]. Developing fluorescent sensors that exploit this phenomenon presents a significant challenge: identifying rapid and highly selective reactions. When a quencher is introduced to fluorescent nanomaterials, a non-radiative energy transfer occurs, which can be explained by various mechanisms. One notable mechanism is FRET, in which energy is transferred from an excited donor to a nearby acceptor via dipole-dipole interactions, without emitting radiation [155]. FRET operates through a dynamic quenching process, allowing for the efficient quenching of fluorescence by inorganic ions. The target analyte's presence can restore the fluorescence, providing the fundamental mechanism for detection. Many researchers have explored strategies to enhance the sensing of target substances using this principle. However, developing new methods that can distinguish between analytes with similar structures remains a challenge [156,157]. Recent studies have demonstrated the potential of fluorescent nanomaterials for specific biothiol identification. For example, a biothiol sensing mechanism developed by Khan and Patil utilizes C-dots or GQDs for detection. This approach showcases the potential of these nanomaterials for targeted biothiol detection. Further research is needed to develop novel methods that can selectively detect analytes with similar structures, ultimately enabling the creation of more effective and accurate fluorescent sensors (Fig. 3 ) [91].

The production of C-dots and GQDs is broadly categorized into two main approaches: top-down and bottom-up techniques. These approaches differ fundamentally in the way the carbon-based structures are prepared [158]. Top-down approaches involve fragmenting larger carbon-based materials into smaller units using physical processes like laser ablation or electrochemical methods. Conversley, bottom-up approaches involve building C-dots and GQDs from molecular precursors derived from various carbon sources. Various synthesis techniques can be used similar to bottom-up and top down methods, including electrochemical carbonization [159], chemical ablation [160], laser ablation [161], microwave irradiation [162], pyrolysis [163], and hydrothermal/solvothermal techniques [164]. Techniques like surface passivation, doping, and functionalization can be utilized for surface modification of C-dots or GQDs, either during their synthesis or as a post-synthesis treatment [165]. Fig. 4 illustrates the preparation methods and potential applications of C-dots and GQDs through both top-down and bottom-up methods. While numerous fabrication strategies for C-dots [166] and GQDs [154,167] have been reviewed extensively in previous research, this section will highlight recent developments and progress in the synthesis and applications of these nanomaterials.

Heavy metal ions pose significant environmental and health risks, and there is a growing need for efficient and selective detection methods. Carbon-based fluorescent nanomaterials, such as C-Dots and GQDs, have shown great promise in this regard thanks to their vibrant and size-variable luminescence properties [168]. Researchers have developed a novel optical nano-sensor using GQDs and C-Dots to detect heavy metal ions. The sensor utilizes a FRET mechanism between GQDs and C-dots, facilitating precise detection of heavy metal ions with remarkable selectivity. The research highlighted the effectiveness of this process, showcasing the sensor's exceptional ability to detect arsenic ions (As5+) with high sensitivity [168]. Furthermore, the study focused on the synthesis of C-Dots and GO using a modified process. The morphology of the C-Dots and GQDs was assessed using advanced microscopy techniques, and the energy transfer dynamics between GQDs and C-Dots were studied using time-resolved photo-luminescence spectroscopy [168]. Additionally, researchers have developed a novel nano-sensor for detecting thiazide (TH) drugs using a range of materials, including glutathione, Tris hydroxymethyl aminomethane, and CdCl 2.5H2O.

The nano-sensor was optimized using the Bos-Behnken design approach and achieved high and stable fluorescence intensity when modified with the OS-g-PDMAEMA biopolymer. This modification with the biopolymer enhanced the fluorescence intensity of the GSH-CdTe/ZnS QDs. The addition of TH caused the intensity of fluorescence on the nano-sensor to decrease. The highly selective and sensitive detection of TH in aqueous solutions, with an optimal pH range that was determined to be 7–7.5, was the intended design for the nano-sensor. Notably, the fluorescence quenching intensity was able to remain stable 60 min after the introduction of TH. The sensor showed excellent TH recovery rates, ranging from 98.66 % to 103.26 %, with relative standard deviations (RSDs) below 4 % [82]. In another study done by Hooshyar et al. they synthesized GSH-CdTe/ZnS QDs using cationic starch (CS) and thioglycolic acid as crucial components. The optical properties of the QDs were examined, revealing that the fluorescence intensity varied notably with increasing CS concentrations. The study also demonstrated the potential of CS-GSH-CdTe/ZnS QDs as a fluorescence nanosensor for biological systems imaging. The study also investigated the fluorescence intensity of CS-GSH-CdTe/ZnS QDs as a potential probe for detecting rifampicin (Rif). The results showed significant dampening of the fluorescence intensity after adding various concentrations of Rif. The study demonstrated the reliability and suitability of the proposed nano-sensor in quantifying Rif within commercial capsules and tablets [169]. Overall, these studies demonstrate the potential of carbon-based fluorescent nanomaterials and QDs in detecting heavy metal ions and pharmaceuticals. The developed nano-sensors show remarkable sensitivity, fast response times, cost-effectiveness, and structural stability, paving the way for innovative QD-based detection technologies [169].

GQDs have been extensively researched in recent years, with several notable studies demonstrating their potential as fluorescent probes for various applications. One such study by Safardoust-Hojaghan et al. reported the synthesis of sulfur and nitrogen co-doped graphene QDs (S,N-GQDs) using citric acid, ethylene diamine, copper acetate, and thiourea. The study demonstrated that the S,N-GQDs can be used as a probe for detecting AA through an off-on fluorescent sensor, with a detection limit of 1.2 μM at a signal-to-noise ratio of 3. The S,N-GQDs were found to be uniform in size and showed excellent stability and sensitivity for AA detection [95].

Wang et al. investigated the cytotoxicity of GQDs-MnO2 nanocomposite using in vitro cell viability tests and cell imaging. The nanocomposite was synthesized using citric acid pyrolysis and further developed using a simultaneous redox reaction assisted by ultrasonication with poly(allylamine hydrochloride) (PAH). During the ultrasonication process with PAH], the GQDs-MnO2 nanocomposite was fabricated to function as a fluorescent nanosensor for detecting GSH. The study revealed a direct relationship between fluorescence recovery efficiency and GSH levels. This sensor demonstrated a remarkable detection limit of only 48 nM, along with exceptional stability and high sensitivity for GSH detection [171]. Yan et al. developed a novel fluorescence-based nanosensor incorporating GQDs and MnO2 nanosheets for the precise and efficient detection of GSH [170]. Their work highlights the dual role of MnO2 nanosheets as fluorescence quenchers and GSH recognizers, which, upon interaction with GSH, undergo reduction to Mn2+ ions, resulting in the restoration of fluorescence in GQDs. This "turn-off-on" mechanism allows for high specificity and sensitivity, making the platform suitable for intracellular imaging and potential applications in diagnosing GSH-associated diseases. By employing simple, cost-effective synthesis methods, the authors demonstrated the nanosensor's capability to monitor GSH levels in living cells with remarkable efficiency and selectivity. Fig. 5 represents the synthesis, structural characterization, and sensing mechanism of GQDs and MnO2 nanosheets, with a particular focus on their application in detecting GSH. In another study done by Dong et al. they focused on the preparation of fluorescent GQDs and GO nanomaterials by tuning the carbonization degree of carbonized carbon. The GQDs were found to be mostly nano-sheets of 15 nm in size, with a height distributed in the range of 0.5–2.0 nm. Both GQDs and GO were found to exhibit strong and excitation-independent PL activity, with the GQDs emitting blue light (460 nm) with a nearly excitation-independent emission wavelength. The study demonstrated that the GQDs and GO nanomaterials can be used as fluorescent probes for various applications, including sensing and imaging [172].

The synthesis and application of carbon-based nanomaterials, such as C-dots and fluorescent nanocrystals (NC-dots), for different applications, such as bio-imaging and sensing. Komalavalli et al. reported the synthesis of C-dots from Hibiscus sabdariffa, a plant with potential biological applications. The researchers extracted the plant material and hydrothermally treated it to obtain fluorescent C-dots. The C-dots were analyzed through multiple techniques, including IR, TEM, UV, and photoluminescence spectral studies, cytotoxicity, and bio-cell imaging. The analysis indicated that the C-dots ranged in size from 4.85 to 7.78 nm and exhibited a spherical morphology. The optical properties of the C-dots were examined through UV–visible spectroscopy and photoluminescence analysis, which revealed intense blue fluorescence emission. The C-dots were also tested for their potential as bio-imaging agents and Cr(VI) sensors. The results demonstrated that the C-dots were non-toxic and showed excellent bio-imaging efficiency, with blue, red, and green fluorescent emission when subjected to different wavelengths of light [173].

Nandi et al. synthesized amphiphilic C-dots designed for spectroscopic studies and multicolor visualization of cellular membranes. By leveraging FRET, these C-dots functioned as energy donors to membrane-associated dyes, enabling the visualization of bilayer reorganization and interactions with membrane-active agents. Their study highlighted the exceptional photoluminescence range, stability, and biocompatibility of these dots, making them a versatile tool for imaging and membrane analysis. Fig. 6 showcases the application of these amphiphilic C-dots in cell imaging, demonstrating their uniform distribution within cellular structures, confirming their effectiveness as imaging agents for biological systems [174]. After incubating cells with C-dots for 4 h, they exhibited blue, red, and green fluorescence when subjected to wavelengths of 330 nm, 450 nm, and 510 nm, respectively. Observations revealed that the C-dots were primarily localized within the cell membrane and cytoplasm, with no significant presence in the cell nuclei. This indicates that the C-dots established effective interactions with the cells while maintaining their viability. As a result, C-dots show great potential as effective bio-imaging agents. Another study by Das et al. proposed the synthesis of fluorescent NC-dots using lemon juice and l-arginine. The NC-dots were prepared through thermal coupling and characterized using HRTEM. The results demonstrated that the NC-dots emitted broad spectra, indicating their fluorescent character. The study further examined the impact of dissolved oxygen on the NC-dots solution and found that the quantum yield value was 7.7 %. The fluorescence intensity of the NC-dots was sensitive to Cu2+ concentration, and they exhibited strong fluorescence intensity in acidic solutions and weakly acidic media. The findings suggest that the NC-dots are great for environmental detection. The synthesis of C-dots and NC-dots using natural materials, such as plant extracts and fruit juices, is an innovative approach to developing sustainable and environmentally friendly nanomaterials. Leveraging these materials for bio-imaging and sensing applications is anticipated to greatly influence advancements in biomedical research and environmental monitoring [139].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Carbon quantum dots based on inner filter effect
ce:section.ce:para: Khezri et al. studied a dual-mode nanosensor leveraging the IFE, where gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) influenced the fluorescence of CdS QDs for the precise detection of arginine. The research focused on the characteristics of citrate-stabilized AuNPs in colloidal form and CdS QDs capped with L-Cys. AuNPs show strong plasmonic absorption at 524 nm, appearing red, while L-Cys-capped CdS QDs exhibit peak fluorescence at 560 nm. FTIR studies show resemblance, with AuNPs overlapping with CdS QDs' fluorescence emission spectrum. The fluorescence decrease is attributed to AuNPs' IFE on CdS QDs' fluorescence. This method offers enhanced signals and lower detection limits, making it a label-free, accurate, and fast method for arginine detection [175]. The study analyzed Cys-capped CdS QDs using fluorimetry and absorption spectrophotometry. The synthesized QDs exhibited a broad absorption band, wide emission spectrum, and symmetric, narrow fluorescence spectra. Optimal reaction conditions, including time and temperature, were determined for detecting ceftriaxone using the Cys-CdS QDs. The selectivity of the QDs as a highly specific probe was assessed, with fluorescence intensity decreasing in response to varying concentrations of ceftriaxone. The proposed sensor demonstrated excellent recovery rates for ceftriaxone detection, confirming the method's reliability for direct analysis. These findings establish the Cys-CdS QDs as an effective and sensitive tool for rapid ceftriaxone detection [176].

Förster Resonance Energy Transfer (FRET) is a photophysical mechanism describing the transfer of energy between two light-sensitive molecules, known as chromophores. This process occurs through non-radiative dipole–dipole coupling and is particularly sensitive to the spatial proximity of the interacting molecules, generally within a range of 1–10 nm. In a typical FRET interaction, the donor chromophore, upon excitation transfers its energy directly to a nearby acceptor chromophore without photon emission. The efficiency of this energy transfer is influenced by several factors [177–181]. These include the distance between the donor and acceptor molecules, the degree of spectral overlap between the donor's emission and the acceptor's absorption spectra, and the relative orientation of their dipole moments. FRET is widely utilized in chemical and biological research as a non-invasive method to study molecular interactions, enabling the monitoring of dynamic processes and intermolecular distances within living cells or tissues [181,182].

Fluorescence quenching refers to the reduction in fluorescence intensity of a molecule (fluorophore) due to various interactions with other molecules, commonly referred to as quenchers. These quenching processes may occur naturally or be induced intentionally for analytical and diagnostic purposes. One of the primary quenching mechanisms is dynamic (or collisional) quenching, in which the quencher interacts with the fluorophore during its excited state. This interaction facilitates non-radiative relaxation, thereby suppressing photon emission. A well-known example of dynamic quenching is the interaction between oxygen molecules and excited fluorophores, where collisional encounters lead to a measurable decrease in fluorescence [183].

In contrast, static quenching involves the formation of a stable, non-fluorescent complex between the fluorophore and the quencher in the ground state. In this case, the reduction in fluorescence does not depend on the excited state of the fluorophore. A common example includes the formation of ground-state complexes between fluorophores and metal ions such as lead or mercury [184].

Other notable quenching mechanisms include energy transfer-based quenching, such as FRET, which occurs via dipole–dipole coupling between donor and acceptor molecules; photoinduced electron transfer, where an electron is transferred from the excited fluorophore to the quencher; and Dexter electron transfer, which requires direct orbital overlap between the fluorophore and quencher, resulting in non-radiative decay [185].

Fluorescence quenching has wide-ranging applications across scientific disciplines. In biochemistry, it is used to study molecular interactions, including binding affinities and conformational changes, and to monitor biochemical processes such as protein folding and enzyme activity. In medical diagnostics, quenching mechanisms are employed in the development of fluorescence-based assays for detecting key biomolecules, including nucleic acids and proteins. Additionally, in environmental science, fluorescence quenching techniques are instrumental in detecting oxygen, heavy metals, and various pollutants in environmental samples [185–189].

The "turn-off/on" mechanisms in fluorescent sensors play a crucial role in detecting specific analytes by modulating fluorescence signals in response to target interactions. These mechanisms are particularly valuable in environmental monitoring, where sensitive detection of contaminants like heavy metals is essential. In "turn-off" sensors, fluorescence is quenched when a target analyte interacts with the sensor's surface, altering its electron-hole recombination process. This quenching effect enables precise measurement of analyte concentration. Conversely, "turn-on" sensors restore or enhance fluorescence upon interaction with specific target molecules, which is often accomplished by attaching specific ligands to QDs or surface modifications. Both approaches leverage the unique surface chemistry of QDs and their responsive fluorescence properties, providing robust and selective sensing methods that address challenges in environtal and biological detection [190,191].

In certain situations, arsenic ions can significantly enhance the emission signals through a turn on mechanism. CQDs with dithiothreitol (DTT) employ S–S chemistry to introduce SH groups on their surface. This modification facilitates the interactions of arsenic and sulfur. This allows detection of As(V) ions with a detection limit as low as 0.086 ppb [192]. For QDs the choice of coating agent is highly important in determing the dispersion properties. The coating agent used was Cysteamine for its dual functionality. The thiol groups effectively bind to As(III) ions, while the hydrophilic amine groups improve water solutbility of CdTe/ZnS core-shell QDs. These cysteamine-functionalized QDs have positive charges, giving them electrostatic interactions with negatively charged aptamers. The QDs will now be able to exhibit neutral surface charges, reduced repulsion, and aggregation promotion, that wil lead to fluorescence quenching. Upon introduction of As (III) ions, they bind to the aptamer, which disrupts the aggregation of QDs and resulting in fluorescence enhancement. Using this technique achieves a detection limit of 0.0013 ppb [193]. Also, co-doping of the C-dots with nitrogen and cerium (CDN/Ce) has been utilized for arsenic sensing. The interaction between As(III) ions and aptamers with the partially quenched blue fluorescence of CDN/Ce through electrostatic forces, restores and amplifies the fluorescence signals as As(III) ions concetration increases. This approach has been shown to detect As(III) ions with a detection limit as low as 0.2 ppb [194].

Wang et al. pioneered the development of a new composite material combining square-plate-like MnO2 nanomaterials enveloped by GQDs, functioning as a fluorescent switch-on sensor for detecting GSH levels as shown in Fig. 7 . MnO2 nanomaterials are prized for their exceptional light absorption and electron transfer properties, making them valuable components in fluorescent nanosensor applications. For the development of fluorescent "switch-off-on" nanosensors various researchers have investigated a range of fluorescent substances, such as carbon dots, gold nanoclusters, copper nanoclusters, metal complexes, silica nanospheres, boron nitride QDs, and up-conversion nanoparticles, to incorporate with MnO2 nanomaterials. In this scenario, the nanoscale MnO2 play two roles: acting as quenchers of fluorescence and aiding in the identification of GSH through a unique redox process. Building upon the initial use of MnO2 nanosheet-upconversion nanomaterial combinations for detecting intracellular glutathione, diverse forms of MnO2 nanomaterials have been employed to develop glutathione-responsive fluorescent switch-on sensors, both in laboratory and biological settings. Effective fluorescence quenching necessitates close proximity between MnO2 and the fluorescent substance, with electrostatic interactions and chemical bonding playing crucial roles in the construction of MnO2-based nanosensors. Specifically designed C-dots have been created to bind with nanoscale MnO2 through electrostatic interactions and amid bond formation reactions in the context of GSH detection [171].

The fluorescence properties of QDs are greatly influenced by their surface states. Interactions between target molecules and QDs affect the efficiency of the electron-hole recombination process, which is critical for their function as sensors [195]. Surface functionalization of nanomaterials with selective ligands has been used for arsenic ion detection. For instance, GSH has been employed to modify the surface of 4–5 nm QDs. Functionalization of QDs derived from prickly pear cactus has shown improved selectivity and sensitivity in sensing applications. The activation of surface groups such as amine, hydroxyl, and carboxyl has enabled the detection of arsenic ions at concentrations as low as 2.3 nM [196]. Significant advancements have been made in developing fluorescent probes for arsenic ion detection using materials like QDs, zinc oxide (ZnO), and carbon-based nanostructures. One method combines ZnO with QDs to leverage the unique electronic properties of nano-sized ZnO. These ZnO/QDs demonstrate efficient transitions between the valence and conduction bands with minimal absorption damping when exposed to increasing concentrations of arsenic species. This enables detection of As (III), As(V), and total arsenic at limits of 27, 7, and 28 ppb, respectively [197]. Another strategy involves doping ZnO with curcumin, which forms a chelation bond with zinc and enhances exciton emission. This approach effectively detects arsenic over a wide concentration range, from 1 to 3000 ppb. Curcumin's enol structure facilitates bonding with zinc, and its integration on the ZnO surface reduces defects, serving as luminescence centers and extending electron-hole recombination. Moreover, l-cysteine (L-Cys) coated green luminescent, water-soluble CdTe-QDs have been applied for detecting As (III) at concentrations as low as 2.0 nM. This detection mechanism involves the displacement of the L-Cys layer when exposed to As (III) ions, which creates surface defects on CdTe-QDs and leads to fluorescence quenching [198]. A distinct type of luminescent material, cerium(III)-based coordination polymer nanoparticles (Ce-CPNs), has been utilized for the detection of As(III) ions at concentrations as low as 0.7 ppb. The uncoordinated carboxyl and para-methylthio groups in Ce-CPNs were observed to interact with As(III) ions via As–S and As–O bond formation. This interaction causes a linear decrease in fluorescence intensity as the concentration of As(III) ions increases [199].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sulfur-infused graphene QDs for improved fluorescent sensing capabilities
ce:section.ce:para: Sulfur-doped GQDs (S-GQDs) stand out owing to their extensive surface area and rich presence of carboxylic groups distributed across their surface and edges, allowing for effective interaction with various functional chemicals [200]. While S-GQDs have a lower quantum yield than toxic inorganic QDs, the incorporation of elemental doping presents a promising approach to enhance their quantum yield. This can be accomplished by adding localized states that alter their electron transfer characteristics, resolving issues like self-quenching, and increasing their possible uses in fields like energy storage, sensors, and catalysis [201–205]. There is a pressing need to develop straightforward and swift techniques that employ fluorescent markers to enable highly sensitive monitoring of pesticide residues using S-GQDs, which are less expensive and hazardous. Nair et al. produced S-GQDs from GO by employing KMnO4 as an oxidant and H2SO4 as a sulfur dopant via a microwave-assisted ultrasonic process at 90 °C for 5 min. The synthesized S-GQDs achieved a quantum efficiency of 27.8 % and a production output of 85.0 % while also displaying an extended lifespan. These S-GQDs exhibited improved crystallinity, a uniform size distribution (∼5 nm), as well as a graphene thickness of a monolayer. Moreover, the S-GQDs demonstrated a flexible color transition from blue to green in comparison toto non-doped GQDs [206].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence quenching detection utilizing Hg2+-Graphene QDs
ce:section.ce:para: Sricharoen et al. explored a novel approach to repurpose biowaste rich in calcium and phosphorus, sourced from Nile tilapia fish scales, into nano-hydroxyapatite (FHAP). The methodology encompassed the ultrasonic-assisted extraction of calcium and phosphorus from the fish scales, with a meticulous optimization process involving acid concentration, ultrasonic power, and extraction time and temperature [207]. GQDs find widespread application as selective fluorescent probes for detecting mercury ions (Hg2+) due to their simple, rapid, and non-toxic nature [208,209]. Belonging to the category of zero-dimensional carbon-based materials, these nanostructures are primarily composed of single or few-layer graphene sheets with lateral dimensions falling below 100 nnm [209]. The creation of GQDs involved a pyrolysis method utilizing citric acid as the initial material [210,211]. The elevated adsorption capacity seen could be linked to the heightened ultrasonic power applied. The energy transmitted by ultrasonic waves has the potential to alter the adsorption balance by aiding mass transfer through the hydrodynamic alterations produced by ultrasonic cavitation [212].

Paper-based fluorescent sensors (PFSs) have emerged as powerful tools for on-site detection of biochemical and environmental analytes, offering advantages such as portability, low cost, and visual readout without the need for complex instrumentation. Building on these strengths, Chang et al. [48] developed a PFS for the rapid and visual detection of acetylcholinesterase (AChE) activity and the organophosphate pesticide diazinon, based on a highly sensitive “turn-on” fluorescence mechanism. The sensor utilizes the aggregation-induced emission (AIE) effect of tetraphenylethylene (TPE), functionalized with a maleimide group (TPE-M). Upon AChE catalyzed hydrolysis of acetylthiocholine (ATCh), the resulting thiocholine reacts with TPE-M to form the fluorescent adduct TPE-M-S, restoring fluorescence through the AIE mechanism and enabling naked-eye detection under 365 nm UV light. This platform also allows indirect detection of OPs like diazinon, which inhibit AChE and suppress fluorescence generation. The assay achieved detection limits of 2.5 mU/mL for AChE and 0.5 ng/mL for diazinon on paper, and down to 0.23 ng L−1 in solution. The sensor maintained strong performance in diluted human serum samples, demonstrating excellent reproducibility and selectivity. As illustrated in Fig. 8 , the system's mechanism and visual output are clearly depicted. The solid-state format, low fabrication cost, and user-friendly operation highlight the potential of this AIE-based PFS for point-of-care diagnostics and environmental screening [48].

Beyond environmental toxins, the versatility of PFS technology has extended into disease diagnostics. He et al. introduced a double-layered PFS specifically designed for the early screening of oral squamous cell carcinoma (OSCC) through the detection of salivary biomarkers such as lactic acid and choline. The sensor integrates a top reaction layer which is loaded with selective oxidases like lactate oxidase and choline oxidase, and a bottom fluorescent detection layer embedded with a porphine-based polymer colloid (PF-PDMTP) and hydroquinone (HQ). Upon enzymatic oxidation of the target substrates, hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) is produced, which reacts with the fluorescent polymer to induce a detectable color shift from red to blue. This shift is quantifiable via a smartphone-based grayscale analysis system, enabling semi-quantitative and highly accessible detection. The sensor demonstrated strong linearity (R2 ≥ 0.98) with detection limits of 5.7 μM for lactic acid and 8.9 μM for choline. Its validation in artificial and clinical saliva samples highlights its promise as a rapid (∼5 min), low-cost, and portable diagnostic tool for early-stage cancer detection [213].

To further contextualize the role of paper-based platforms in non-invasive disease diagnostics, Cong Chen et al. [214] comprehensively reviewed the recent progress in paper-based fluorescent biosensors for salivary biomarker detection, emphasizing their advantages for point-of-care applications. These sensors, including dipstick assays, lateral flow assays, and microfluidic analytical devices, leverage the low cost, flexibility, and capillary-driven flow properties of paper to provide rapid, non-invasive diagnostics. They have been effectively integrated with signal amplification strategies, smartphone-based quantification, and multiplexing designs to enhance sensitivity and user accessibility. Particularly in the context of saliva analysis, these devices enable real-time monitoring of biomarkers associated with diseases such as COVID-19, oral cancers, and metabolic disorders, offering a practical platform for decentralized health screening and early diagnosis.

Expanding beyond enzymatic sensing and disease diagnostics, paper-based fluorescence platforms have also been adapted for the detection of pesticide residues through competitive binding and quenching mechanisms. Chen et al. [51] have introduced a novel fluorescence-based detection method for carbamate pesticides using a paper sensor. This innovative approach combines CdTe QDs with nano zinc 5,10,15,20-tetrakis(4-pyridyl)-21H–23H-porphyrin (nano ZnTPyP), a unique fluorescence suppressor synthesized using a cationic surfactant. The positively charged nano ZnTPyP binds to the QD's surface, quenching their fluorescence. When a negatively charged carbamate pesticide is introduced, it interacts with the nano ZnTPyP, causing it to detach from the QDs and restore their fluorescence. This phenomenon enables detecting of carbamate pesticides (CPs) with a sensitivity comparable to conventional chromatographic methods. The proposed sensor has several advantages, including rapid detection, high sensitivity, cost-effectiveness, and equipment-free operation. Moreover, it can be easily integrated into paper-based chips, making it an attractive solution for on-site monitoring of CPs in complex vegetable, fruit, and tea samples.

Beyond salivary diagnostics, paper-based biosensors have also been applied to infectious disease detection and antimicrobial resistance (AMR) surveillance. Laliwala et al. [215] provided a comprehensive overview of paper-based biosensors for the detection of AMR bacterial species, emphasizing their role as point-of-care diagnostic tools. These platforms leverage the unique morphological and chemical properties of paper, such as porosity, hydrophilicity, and surface modifiability to support fluorometric detection. Paper-based sensors enable rapid, low-cost, and equipment-free identification of pathogens by integrating fluorescent dyes, enzymes, or nucleic acid probes. Their adaptability, ease of fabrication, and compatibility with microfluidics make them especially suitable for resource-limited settings, where conventional diagnostic infrastructure is unavailable. Such biosensors hold promise for improving early diagnosis and antibiotic stewardship.

Environmental sensing is another prominent application area for PFS technology, particularly in the detection of heavy metals and pollutants. Suo et al. [216] developed a paper-based fluorescent FRET aptasensor for high-throughput detection of lead ions (Pb2+) in real-world samples such as water, soil, and food. The sensor uses a Pb2+-specific aptamer that forms a G-quadruplex in the presence of Pb2+, preventing hybridization with complementary DNA labeled with a quencher, thereby restoring fluorescence. This mechanism enabled sensitive detection within a linear range of 0.01–10 μM and a low detection limit of 6.1 nM. The device showed strong performance in complex matrices, excellent reproducibility, and suitability for rapid, on-site environmental monitoring and food safety applications using a 96-well microplate format.

In addition to environmental pollutants, PFSs have been developed for nutritional and clinical metabolite monitoring. Li et al. [217] developed a FPS for the detection of folic acid (FA), utilizing CDs covalently anchored onto cellulose paper via Schiff base chemistry. The detection mechanism is based on a fluorescence “turn-off” response, where FA induces quenching through an inner filter effect. The sensor demonstrated high sensitivity, with a detection limit of 0.28 μmol L−1 across a wide range of 1–300 μmol L−1. Its performance in real samples, including orange juice and urine, showed excellent recovery and reproducibility, confirming its utility as a low-cost, stable, and effective platform for clinical and nutritional monitoring.

Similarly, Assi et al. explored the detection of thiol-containing metabolites in food matrices using integrated microfluidic-paper platforms [218]. They introduced a novel microfluidic paper-based fluorescent sensor (μPADs@MIPs@QDs) for the selective detection of l-homocysteine in complex matrices like white wine. Their design integrates molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) on paper to selectively capture the target thiol, followed by UV-induced in situ synthesis of ZnCdCu/S quantum dots directly on the paper surface. The resulting fluorescence signal is proportional to the l-homocysteine concentration, enabling quantitative analysis with a detection limit of 0.51 μM. This eco-friendly, low-cost platform offers improved selectivity, reproducibility, and field-deployability for thiol monitoring in both food safety and biomedical applications.

Expanding the functionality of PFSs even further, Zhu et al. [219] developed a multifunctional paper-based fluorescent sensor array for rapid bacterial identification and inactivation using metal-doped multicolor CQDs. By integrating Ag-, Cu-, and Zn-doped CQDs onto filter paper through inkjet printing, the platform achieved simultaneous sensing and antibacterial action. Bacterial presence induced fluorescence quenching, with specific patterns enabling differentiation of five bacterial species via machine learning analysis. The sensor demonstrated excellent sensitivity, detecting concentrations as low as 103 CFU/mL, and achieved classification accuracies of up to 100 %. Additionally, the doped CQDs exhibited strong bactericidal activity, effectively damaging bacterial membranes without additional treatments. This portable, cost-effective system offers significant promise for point-of-care diagnostics and environmental monitoring.

PFS have demonstrated remarkable potential in both environmental and biomedical domains. From detecting environmental contaminants such as pesticides, heavy metals, and pathogens, to enabling rapid, non-invasive diagnostics through salivary and clinical biomarkers, these platforms offer a compelling combination of sensitivity, simplicity, and portability. Their adaptability to various detection strategies and integration with nanomaterials and enzymatic systems make them highly suitable for point-of-care and field-based applications. Building on this foundation, the next section explores ratiometric fluorescence sensors, which are an emerging class of devices that further enhance analytical reliability by incorporating internal referencing strategies for more accurate, quantitative detection in complex sample matrices.

While the majority of fluorescence tests typically concentrate on either diminishing or enhancing fluorescence for detection, these individual responses are susceptible to various experimental variables. Conversely, utilizing ratiometric fluorescence not only boosts measurement precision but also significantly minimizes external disturbances. Hence, a straightforward ratiometric fluorescent detector, first developed by Yao et al., enables highly accurate and sensitive identification of OPs. This biosensor hinges on inhibiting the enzymatic etching process of MnO2 nanosheets and leveraging the distinct reactivity of two fluorescent compounds with MnO2 nanosheets. AChE triggers the conversion of acetyl thiol choline (ATCh) substrate into choline (TCh), which subsequently reduces MnO2 nanosheets to Mn2+. OPs hinder AChE activity, halting TCh production and subsequently preventing MnO2 nanosheets decomposition. Acting as the identifier, MnO2 nanosheets induce alterations in fluorescence intensity towards SC and AR. By capitalizing on the distinct fluorescence responses "Off" and "On" of these two signal molecules triggered by OPs, this effective ratiometric fluorescence system with MnO2 nanosheets enable OP detection [220]. Han et al. developed a dual-responsive ratiometric fluorescence sensor by coordinating europium ions (Eu3+) with cadmium telluride QDs (CdTe QDs) to enable rapid and visually detectable sensing of tetracycline (TC). When TC is introduced, the sensor's fluorescence undergoes a distinct color shift from green to yellow and eventually to red, offering a dose-dependent and wide-ranging chromatic detection approach for TC. This method demonstrates significant potential for practical applications, aligning with the principles of simplicity, speed, visual detection, and real-time monitoring, making it highly relevant for food safety and environmental protection [221]. Furthermore, the fluorescence emission from trivalent europium ions (Eu3+) exhibits distinctive spectroscopic properties, such as a large Stokes shift, extended fluorescence lifetime, and well-defined narrow emission peaks similar to spectral lines (Fig. 9 ) [222]. Nevertheless, the Eu-TC complex displays relatively weak fluorescence, primarily due to the quenching effects induced by the vibrational energy of coordinated water molecules [223]. Furthermore, these sensor platforms rely on europium face constraints in practical usage due to their instability in moist surroundings and when exposed to ultraviolet light [224].

Utilizing suitable chelating agents like citric acid [225], Triton X-100 [226], and coordination polymers [227], can amplify the detection capability of the Eu-TC sensor. However, mono-emission "off-on" fluorescent probes based on Eu3+ often prove to be quite unreliable as they are significantly impacted by environmental factors, instrument efficacy, and excitation mechanisms. This unreliability arises from the fact that these measurements solely depend on the fluctuations in the emission intensity of Eu3+, which may not always be highly consistent [221]. Wang et al. conducted research on a double-emission CdTe QD@ZIF-365 ratiometric fluorescent sensor to detect l-histidine and Cu2+ ions with exceptional signal response [228]. In recent times, metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) have surfaced as an inventive hybrid material type consisting of organic ligands and metal ions or clusters. MOFs have showcased versatility across diverse applications such as gas separation, drug delivery, and fluorescent sensing owing to their extensive aromatic backbones with distinct photoluminescent characteristics, which make them highly suitable for fluorescence sensor development [229]. Moreover, the aromatic conjugated frameworks of MOFs produce intense photoluminescence, rendering them excellent candidates for fluorescent sensor applications [230]. An exceptional category within MOFs, zeolitic imidazolate frameworks (ZIFs), has emerged as promising materials owing to their unique tetrahedral structures and adaptable functionalities [231]. ZIFs are recognized for their adjustable pore sizes, exceptional stability, and structures akin to inorganic zeolites [232]. To enhance the performance of MOF-based fluorescent sensors, incorporating highly luminescent guest molecules into ZIFs proves effective [233]. QDs provide advantages over traditional luminescent materials like organic dyes and rare-earth ions due to their unmatched photophysical and chemical traits including broad absorption spectra, extended lifetimes, symmetric emission bands, minimal photobleaching, and high quantum yields [234,235]. However, in various environments, biomolecules can interact with QDs, leading to the formation of surface defects and imperfections in their crystalline structure. These defects disrupt the structural integrity of QDs, which directly results in fluorescence quenching [236,237]. Implementing protective coating with an additional layer and suitable band gap to form core-shell structures stands as a potent approach to enhance fluorescence quantum yield and stability of QDs against photo-oxidation [238]. Embedding QDs within metal-organic frameworks post-synthesis can broaden their applications. QDs@MOFs-based hybrid materials present advantages for developing superior ratiometric fluorescent sensors by preventing fluorescence quenching, enhancing quantum yield and stability, and reducing external interferences in detection procedures compared to MOFs-based sensors with single chromophore emission. The utilization of two independent emissions' fluorescence intensity ratio aids in mitigating these challenges [239–241]. Wang et al. synthesized green fluorescent C-dots@zeolitic imidazolate framework-8 nanoparticles (C-dots@ZIF-8 NPs) with adjustable size and fluorescence output, serving as adaptable systems for targeted drug delivery and fluorescence-based cancer cell imaging [242]. Hence, the design of QDs@MOFs-based ratiometric sensors exhibits significant potential for target molecule detection [228].

The research introduces a fluorescence sensing approach using MnO2 nanosheets, known for their oxidase-like catalytic properties. Scopoletin (SC) and Amplex Red (AR), both fluorescent peroxidase substrates, exhibit differing responses when interacting with MnO2 nanosheets. A ratiometric fluorescent biosensor for OPs detection was constructed by leveraging the inhibition of MnO2 nanosheet enzymatic etching and the fluorogenic properties of SC and AR. The fluorescence intensity ratios of the sensor were evaluated, with the largest ratio recorded at an ATCh concentration of 5 mM. This method enables the sensitive detection of OPs, achieving a limit of detection (LOD) of 1.2 mU/mL. The dual signal strategy proves the sensitivity of OPs detection and provides a novel framework for developing ratiometric fluorescence biosensors construct ratiometric fluorescence biosensors. The sensor's fluorescence intensity ratio was much higher than interferences, indicating good selectivity for distinguishing OPs from other substances [220,243].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Types of nanoparticles used in fluorescent sensors
ce:section.ce:para: Organic-inorganic nanohybrid materials are interconnected through either covalent or noncovalent interactions and have found extensive applications in various fields, such as optoelectronics, catalysis, and biomedicine [244,245]. This unique combination results in nanohybrid materials exhibiting a range of exceptional characteristics, which enhance their stability, multifunctionality, biocompatibility, environmental sustainability, enhanced physical and mechanical attributes. These materials have been utilized in applications such as fluorescence, Raman spectroscopy, photoacoustic imaging, and combined bioimaging [246–248]. Noble metal nanoparticles, including gold (Au) and silver (Ag), are widely studied for their distinctive optical, electrical, magnetic, and catalytic properties. These characteristics make them highly effective as biosensors and as advanced components in optical and electronic devices. In recent years, nanoparticles with precisely defined nanostructures have gained prominence as rapid visual indicators, particularly in colorimetric sensing. This growing interest is driven by their ability to integrate distinctive optical behaviors with exceptional surface and interface recognition abilities. Bimetallic nanoparticles, including Au/Ag [249], Au/Pd [250], and Au/Pt [251], have also become a major focus in the field of sensing, often exhibiting greater sensitivity compared to single-metal nanoparticles. The alloyed structures provide advanced and, in some cases, entirely novel optical, physical, and chemical properties, offering significant advantages for both foundational studies and real-world applications [252,253]. Gold nanomatryoshkas were introduced as an innovative platform for tunable fluorescence enhancement, utilizing Fano resonance effects to optimize light emission characteristics for use in medical imaging and therapeutic procedures. The design combines high biocompatibility with substantial fluorescence amplification, establishing its potential as a cutting-edge tool in nanophotonics. Fig. 10 illustrates the synthesis process and structural characterization of gold nanomatryoshkas, highlighting their multi-layered geometry and the fabrication techniques essential for optimizing their plasmonic properties. This figure systematically demonstrates the step-by-step evolution of the nanomatryoshkas, beginning with the unfunctionalized gold core and progressing through the addition of a silica dielectric layer, functionalization with fluorescent dyes, and the formation of the outer gold shell. Accompanying TEM images validate the structural progression, showcasing precise control over dimensions and morphology at each stage of fabrication [252]. Furthermore, bimetallic nanoparticles exhibit improved activity and selectivity because of their distinct structural and electronic properties comparing to their monometallic components [254]. Studies have shown that bimetallic nanoparticles with precisely engineered nanostructures possess remarkable fluorescence quenching capabilities, which can be attributed to their distinctive optical characteristics and efficient surface recognition abilities [255,256].
Some of the nanoparticles used in the production of fluorescent sensors to detect different materials are given in Table 2 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effective parameters for fluorescence intensity optimization of QD nano sensors
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence signal observed during sensing using QD-based nanosensors is governed by a complex interplay of physicochemical and environmental parameters. These parameters directly affect the interaction dynamics between QDs, target molecules, and surrounding media, ultimately influencing signal strength, specificity, and reproducibility.
