In this context, thirteen key factors are recognized as critical to optimizing fluorescence intensity and analytical performance: pH of the medium, nanoparticle concentration, target concentration, reaction time, ionic strength and buffer composition, temperature, excitation wavelength and light source stability, photobleaching and photostability, surface chemistry and functionalization, FRET efficiency, solvent polarity and viscosity, presence of quenching agents or interferents, and nanoparticle aggregation. A clear understanding of each parameter's contribution allows for precise tuning of assay conditions to achieve high sensitivity, selectivity, and stability in fluorescence-based detection systems.

The pH directly affects the target molecule's charge state and, consequently, its interaction with the nanoparticle surface. When the pH is below the target's isoelectric point, the target's positively charged groups can effectively bind to the negatively charged nanoparticles via electrostatic attraction and hydrogen bonding. However, excessively low pH values introduce non-specific interactions with other charged species, thereby reducing assay selectivity [257,258].

Fluorescence intensity initially increases with nanoparticle concentration, reaching a peak at an optimal threshold. Beyond this threshold, fluorescence begins to decline. This decline is attributed to the excess nanoparticles, which are not sufficiently coated by the target molecules. These exposed nanoparticles can induce quenching effects by bringing QDs into closer proximity, thereby dampening fluorescence. Therefore, fine-tuning nanoparticle concentration is critical for achieving a maximized fluorescence response [175,259,260].

A rise in target concentration results in a steady increase in fluorescence intensity. Concurrently, the plasmon absorption band of nanoparticles shows a gradual decrease, suggesting a consistent interaction between target and nanoparticles. This behavior implies a strong linear correlation between fluorescence intensity and target concentration, which is beneficial for quantitative sensing [261].

The duration of the reaction affects the binding equilibrium between the target molecules and nanoparticles, as well as subsequent nanoparticle-QD interactions. Sufficient reaction time is essential to allow complete binding and stabilization of fluorescence signals [195,262].

The ionic strength of the sensing environment plays a pivotal role in modulating fluorescence signals in QD-based nanosensors. Elevated ionic concentrations can screen electrostatic interactions between negatively charged QDs and their targets, thereby reducing binding efficiency. This screening effect may alter the hybridization behavior of DNA-based probes or induce partial aggregation of nanoparticles, both of which can significantly suppress fluorescence signals. Specific ions such as Cl−, Mg2+, or Na+ may also act as quenchers or promote nonspecific interactions. Liu et al. demonstrated that varying PBS concentrations influenced the charge transfer and morphology of molecular beacon probes, confirming the importance of controlling ionic strength in fluorescence-based nanosensor assays [263].

Temperature is a key parameter influencing the performance of QD-based nanosensors, particularly in the context of fluorescence intensity optimization. It affects several interrelated processes including binding kinetics, fluorophore quantum yield, and nanoparticle stability. While moderately elevated temperatures can accelerate reaction kinetics and improve probe-target hybridization, excessive heat may destabilize the fluorescent probes or the nanoparticle surface, leading to thermal quenching or reduced signal reliability. In the work of Zhang et al. the optimal amplification temperature (55 °C) was identified for achieving maximum fluorescence yield using a single-QD-based nanosensor. Their findings confirm that precise thermal control is crucial for stable and efficient signal generation in fluorescence assays [264].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Excitation wavelength and light source stability
ce:section.ce:para: The excitation wavelength must be carefully matched with the absorption peak of the QD fluorophore to achieve optimal fluorescence output. Inappropriate excitation can result in weak or inconsistent signals. Moreover, the stability of the light source whether a lamp or laser is essential for reliable detection, as fluctuations in intensity or spectral distribution can skew results. Huang et al. [260] systematically evaluated the excitation-dependent behavior of amino-functionalized graphene QDs and optimized the fluorescence output at an excitation wavelength of 346 nm. Their findings underline the necessity of both proper spectral alignment and instrumental stability in achieving reproducible, high-intensity fluorescence signals in QD-based nanosensors.

Photostability refers to the resistance of fluorophores to degradation upon prolonged light exposure. In QD-based nanosensors, photobleaching can lead to a progressive loss of fluorescence intensity, compromising the accuracy and reliability of long-term or real-time sensing applications. While conventional organic dyes often suffer rapid photodegradation, many engineered QDs, especially when protected by surface coatings or encapsulation techniques exhibit remarkable stability under continuous excitation. Fanizza et al. demonstrated that encapsulated giant QDs maintained fluorescence stability during sustained laser exposure, highlighting their suitability for high-fidelity sensing platforms [265].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Surface chemistry and functionalization of nanoparticles
ce:section.ce:para: The surface chemistry of QDs plays a decisive role in determining their binding efficiency, stability, and optical performance in nanosensor systems. Surface ligands, such as PEG derivatives, amphiphilic polymers, or thiol-functionalized small molecules, are employed to enhance water solubility and minimize nonspecific interactions. Bilan et al. [266] emphasized that improper functionalization such as incomplete ligand exchange or insufficient passivation can lead to colloidal instability, nanoparticle aggregation, and background signal artifacts. Moreover, inappropriate surface charge or excessive binding sites may promote unwanted interactions with biological matrices, undermining the fluorescence precision of the sensor. Thus, careful control over QD surface composition is crucial for achieving selective, stable, and reproducible fluorescence-based detection.

In the context of QD-based nanosensors, FRET efficiency plays a pivotal role in determining fluorescence output. Due to their large absorption cross-sections and tunable emission, QDs serve as excellent FRET donors. However, improper donor–acceptor spacing beyond the optimal 1–10 nm range or poor spectral alignment can significantly reduce energy transfer efficiency, leading to weak or inconsistent fluorescence signals. As highlighted by Chou and Dennis [267], optimizing QD surface functionalization and spatial arrangement is essential to maintain high FRET efficiency and ensure reliable signal transduction in these nanosensing platforms.

Both solvent polarity and viscosity critically modulate the optical behavior of QD-based nanosensors. Changes in polarity affect the quantum yield and emission spectra of fluorophores by influencing solvation dynamics and intramolecular charge transfer. More polar environments can induce red or blue shifts and alter emission intensities. Simultaneously, viscosity plays a central role in governing rotational diffusion of molecular rotors and QDs. In low-viscosity media, rapid molecular rotation promotes non-radiative decay pathways, reducing fluorescence. Conversely, high-viscosity environments suppress these pathways, thereby stabilizing the excited state and enhancing fluorescence emission. Yang et al. [268] extensively discussed these phenomena and demonstrated that both solvent polarity and viscosity must be optimized to ensure accurate, reproducible fluorescence in sensing applications.

Fluorescence quenching is a key factor limiting the sensitivity and reliability of QD-based nanosensors. It can occur through dynamic or static mechanisms, each altering QD emission properties. Common interferents include transition metal ions (e.g., Cu2+, Fe3+, Hg2+), oxygen, and biological macromolecules, which can interact with QD surfaces through charge transfer, spectral absorption overlap, or chemical degradation. Lesiak et al. [269] detail how these quenching mechanisms diminish fluorescence intensity and highlight the importance of surface protection and analyte specificity to prevent nonspecific interactions. Understanding and mitigating these interference pathways is essential for optimizing the performance of QD-based fluorescence sensors.

Nanoparticle aggregation can significantly impair the optical performance of QD-based nanosensors. Aggregation often leads to plasmonic coupling between neighboring particles, which can cause shifts in absorption and emission spectra and, more critically, result in fluorescence quenching. The electrostatic binding between QDs and metallic nanoparticles, such as gold, must be finely balanced to avoid undesirable aggregation. Burton et al. [261] demonstrated that while controlled coupling can enhance fluorescence via localized surface plasmon resonance, excess aggregation quenches the fluorescence due to strong near-field interactions. Furthermore, their dynamic light scattering analysis confirmed that monodispersity is vital for achieving reliable and reproducible fluorescence detection. Thus, preventing aggregation is a key design consideration in nanosensor fabrication.

QD sensors are a fascinating area of research, especially for environmental applications. These sensors leverage the unique optical and electronic properties of QDs to detect and monitor environmental contaminants with high sensitivity and specificity. They are used for detecting heavy metals, pesticides, pathogens, and other pollutants in water, air, and soil [270,271]. They offer rapid detection, high sensitivity, and the ability to function in field conditions without xpensive lab equipment [271] while Issues like toxicity, stability, and reproducibility need to be addressed for practical applications [272].

QD sensors are used to detect pollutants like heavy metals (e.g., lead, mercury) and pesticides in water. They offer high sensitivity and rapid detection, making them ideal for real-time monitoring [273,274]. These sensors can identify harmful gases and particulate matter in the air, contributing to better air quality management [275]. They also, help in identifying contaminants like pesticides and heavy metals in soil, aiding in agricultural and environmental conservation [274]. QDs are used as photocatalysts to degrade pollutants in water and air, leveraging their unique optical properties. Light Detection and Ranging (LIDAR) Systems: QDs are being explored for use in LIDAR systems, which are crucial for environmental mapping and monitoring [276,277]. For instance, carbon QD-based sensors have been developed for detecting nitrofen, a harmful pollutant, in aquatic environments [278].

QD sensors are used to detect biomarkers, metal ions, and antibiotics with high sensitivity and specificity. Their fluorescence properties make them ideal for diagnostic tools. They can detect minute quantities of biological molecules. Their tunable optical properties allow for diverse applications and they have a longer fluorescence lifetime compared to traditional dyes [279].

QDs are employed in imaging techniques to visualize cellular and subcellular structures. Their ability to emit multiple fluorescence colors enhances imaging resolution. They are engineered to deliver drugs to specific targets within the body, improving treatment efficacy and reducing side effects. They are used in cancer treatment to generate reactive oxygen species under light exposure, targeting cancer cells and exhibit antibacterial properties, making them useful in combating infections [280]. Taken together, the biomedical applications of QDs span bio-sensing, bio-imaging, drug delivery, Photodynamic Therapy and Anti-Bacterial Activity [279,280].

Applications in public health and safety include pathogen detection, air quality monitoring, food safety, toxicity assessment and environmental monitoring [275,281,282]. QD sensors are used to identify harmful microorganisms like bacteria and viruses in water, air, and food, helping to prevent disease outbreaks [281]. These sensors can detect hazardous gases such as nitrogen dioxide (NO2) and carbon monoxide, contributing to better air quality management [282]. QD-based biosensors are employed to detect contaminants, toxins, and pathogens in food products, ensuring safety and quality [275]. They also, are used to study the toxicity of various substances, aiding in the development of safer materials and chemicals [281] and help track pollutants in the environment, such as heavy metals and pesticides, which have direct implications for public health [275].

Despite the significant advancements in QD-based fluorescence sensors and their proven potential across environmental, biomedical, and industrial domains, several challenges still limit their widespread adoption and long-term sustainability. These challenges stem from technical, economic, and regulatory concerns, which must be addressed to transition QD sensors from laboratory research to real-world deployment. In the following sections, first the current technical limitations of QD-based sensors that hinder their scalability, reliability, and safety in practical applications will be examined. Then the emerging opportunities and future directions will be explored that could help overcome these obstacles and further enhance the performance, accessibility, and impact of QD-based sensing technologies.

QD-based fluorescence sensors are a promising technology for environmental and biomedical detection. However, they face several challenges like toxicity, stability, cost and scalability, selectivity and regulatory hurdles.

Many QDs contain heavy metals like cadmium, which pose environmental and health risks. Developing non-toxic or less toxic alternatives is a priority [283,284]. QDs can degrade under environmental conditions, leading to reduced performance. Improving their stability in various conditions is essential [285,286]. The synthesis of high-quality QDs can be expensive and challenging to scale up for industrial applications [285,287]. While QDs are highly sensitive, achieving high selectivity for specific analytes in complex environments remains a challenge [283,288]. The use of QDs in biomedical applications faces strict regulatory scrutiny due to potential toxicity and long-term effects [284,288].

As the field of QD-based fluorescence sensors continues to mature, a number of promising strategies are emerging to address the limitations currently hindering their broader application. One of the most significant developments is the shift toward green synthesis methods. Traditional QD synthesis often involves toxic solvents and heavy metals, which raise environmental and health concerns. In contrast, green synthesis approaches emphasize the use of non-toxic precursors, aqueous-phase reactions, and energy-efficient conditions. These methods not only reduce environmental impact but also support the development of more biocompatible and regulatory-compliant QDs for medical and environmental use [283,288].

Alongside green chemistry, the engineering of biocompatible QDs represents a key area of innovation. Modifying the QD surface with polymers, biomolecules, or inert coatings such as silica or PEG can significantly reduce cytotoxicity and enhance colloidal stability. These advancements improve the safety profile of QDs, making them more suitable for biomedical applications such as in vivo imaging, targeted drug delivery, and biosensing in clinical settings [284,287].

Another opportunity lies in the creation of hybrid nanomaterials, where QDs are combined with other functional materials such as metal nanoparticles, graphene derivatives, or metal–organic frameworks. These hybrids often exhibit synergistic properties that surpass those of individual components. For instance, hybridization can improve fluorescence intensity, increase stability under physiological conditions, and allow for multi-modal detection strategies. Such materials can expand the range of detectable analytes and enable more robust sensor platforms for real-world deployment [285,286].

The demand for real-time monitoring in both biomedical diagnostics and environmental surveillance is also driving innovation in QD sensor design. By integrating QDs into portable, miniaturized platforms, researchers are enabling the detection of analytes in dynamic and complex settings such as detecting pollutants in water streams or biomarkers in bodily fluids without the need for extensive sample preparation [286,288]. These advancements are particularly valuable in point-of-care testing, environmental risk assessment, and rapid field diagnostics.

Furthermore, the integration of artificial intelligence (AI) into QD sensor systems is emerging as a transformative direction. AI tools can analyze the large datasets generated by multiplexed fluorescence sensors, enabling more accurate pattern recognition, anomaly detection, and decision support. By coupling AI with QD platforms, researchers can automate the interpretation of sensing results, enhance diagnostic precision, and even predict environmental trends or disease outbreaks based on sensor data [286,287].

In summary, these emerging opportunities including, green synthesis, enhanced biocompatibility, hybrid material development, real-time monitoring capability, and AI integration collectively point toward a new generation of QD-based sensors that are not only more sustainable and safe but also smarter and more adaptable. These innovations are expected to play a central role in overcoming existing technical and regulatory barriers, while expanding the scope of applications in medicine, environmental science, agriculture, and beyond. As the field progresses, interdisciplinary collaboration will be essential to fully realize these opportunities and translate laboratory advances into practical, real-world technologies.

The significant advancements in QD-based fluorescence sensors and their transformative potential in environmental monitoring and biomedical applications were investigated. QDs, including C-dots, GQDs, and metal-doped QDs, exhibit exceptional optical properties such as tunable fluorescence, high photostability, and low toxicity, making them ideal for detecting pollutants like heavy metals, pesticides, and pharmaceuticals, as well as for bioimaging and disease diagnosis. Innovative sensing mechanisms, including Förster Resonance Energy Transfer (FRET) and fluorescence quenching, have further enhanced the sensitivity and selectivity of these sensors.

Despite their promising capabilities, challenges such as complex fabrication processes, self-quenching effects, and limited photostability remain barriers to widespread adoption. Future research should focus on improving the stability, reducing production costs, and integrating these sensors with emerging technologies like the Internet of Things (IoT) to enable real-time monitoring and advanced diagnostic tools. By addressing these challenges, QD-based fluorescence sensors have the potential to revolutionize analytical detection technologies and contribute significantly to environmental sustainability and public health safety.
